sections.title: 4.3 Overview of the tri-modal representation learning framework
sections.content: We develop tri-modal representation learning framework that jointly embeds histology images, multiplexed protein images, and text descriptions into shared latent space. Each modality is processed by dedicated encoder followed by modality-specific projection head. All components are trained using contrastive pretraining to enforce cross-modal alignment. Let = {(xHE , xmIF , xTXT )}N i=1 denote the training dataset, where xHE protein (mIF) image patch, and xTXT and clinical attributes. is an H&E image patch, xmIF is the corresponding multiplexed is the associated textual description derived from molecular, spatial, More specifically, let index tissue regions, and let each region contain K(r) patches. The total number of patches satisfies = rR K(r). Linking H&E, mIF, and text in unified framework For each patch index i, we denote by ri the corresponding region and by ki the patch index within that region. Accordingly, xTXT = where all three modalities correspond to the same spatial tissue location. xmIF = xHE = (ri) ki,mIF, (ri) ki,HE, (ri) ki,TXT, Each modality is encoded using modality-specific pretrained encoder: = fHE(xHE hHE ), hmIF = fmIF(xmIF ), hTXT = fTXT(xTXT ). For the H&E modality, we adopt the pretrained MUSK vision transformer as the image encoder. For the mIF modality, as no large-scale publicly available pretrained models exist for multiplexed protein images, we pretrain VirTues encoder from scratch using our private mIF dataset, which includes all available mIF-only slices as well as the training portion of paired slices used for tri-modal alignment. For the text modality, we employ BiomedBERT [26], BERT encoder pretrained from scratch on PubMed abstracts and PubMedCentral full-text articles. In addition, to address the irregularity of biomarker channels in mIF data, we follow the VirTues strategy of encoding each biomarker using its corresponding ESM-3 [57] protein embedding, matched to the protein identity of each biomarker. This design enables the model to accept biomarkers that do not appear in the pretraining set, provided that corresponding ESM embedding is available. For biomarkers such as DAPI that do not have corresponding protein embedding, we initialize learnable embedding during VirTues pretraining and fix this embedding during inference. 4.3.1 Projection heads Each modality-specific encoder output is mapped into shared latent space using projection head: = gm(hm zm ), {HE, mIF, TXT}. All projection heads share the same architecture but maintain independent parameters across modalities. Each projection head is implemented as two-layer multilayer perceptron with output dimension = 512: gm(h) = BN(cid:0)W2 ReLU(W1h)(cid:1). All projected embeddings are ℓ2-normalized prior to contrastive learning: zm = zm zm 2 . 4.3.2 Tri-modal contrastive pretraining Training objective For minibatch of size = 128, the model produces normalized embeddings For any ordered modality pair (a, b), we define contrastive loss {zHE , zmIF , zTXT }B i=1. Lab = 1 i=1 log (cid:16) exp (cid:17) za zb /τ (cid:16) zb za /τ (cid:17) , j=1 exp where similarities are computed using cosine similarity in the shared latent space. The temperature parameter is fixed to τ = 0.07. Losses are computed for all cross-modal pairs and summed to obtain the final training objective: with symmetric counterparts implicitly included. = LHEmIF + LmIFTXT + LHETXT, 24 Linking H&E, mIF, and text in unified framework Optimization and training details Models are trained using the AdamW optimizer. During tri-modal alignment, the H&E and text encoders are fine-tuned in the final two transformer blocks, while the mIF encoder is kept frozen. Distinct learning rates are applied to different parameter groups: 1 105 for the H&E encoder, 2 105 for the text encoder, and 1 104 for all projection heads. two-stage learning rate schedule is employed. Learning rates are linearly warmed up over the first 5,000 optimization steps, followed by cosine annealing for the remainder of training. The model is trained for 25 epochs over the full training dataset.

sections.title: 4.4 Downstream evaluation
sections.content: To comprehensively evaluate the representations learned by Haiku, we assess performance across diverse set of downstream tasks spanning patch-level retrieval, classification, biomarker inference, and slice-level clinical prediction. Unless otherwise specified, all downstream evaluations use frozen pretrained encoders without fine-tuning backbone parameters. For baseline comparisons, we extract unimodal embeddings from MUSK (H&E) and VirTues (mIF) using the backbone outputs after our tri-modal alignment training (i.e., prior to the Haiku projection heads). Throughout the paper, MUSK and VirTues baselines refer to these identically fine-tuned backbones, evaluated under the same splits and downstream protocols as Haiku to ensure fair comparison. 4.4.1 Cross-modality patch-level retrieval We evaluate cross-modality retrieval to assess whether Haiku learns shared latent space in which semantically corresponding tissue patches from different modalities are consistently aligned. In this task, each query corresponds to single patch represented in one modality {HE, mIF, TXT}, and the objective is to retrieve relevant patches from target modality {HE, mIF, TXT}, = a, based on similarity in the learned embedding space. Let denote the held-out evaluation set consisting of spatially aligned patches across all modalities. For each patch H, we obtain modality-specific normalized embeddings where denotes the query modality and denotes the target modality. za , zb , Query and gallery construction. For given retrieval direction b, the query set is defined as and the gallery set is defined as Qa = {za }N i=1, = {zb }N j=1, where the gallery contains all patches from the target modality in the held-out set. Thus, the number of query and gallery embeddings is identical, and retrieval is performed globally across the entire held-out dataset rather than within individual tissue slices. Linking H&E, mIF, and text in unified framework Similarity and ranking. For each query patch Qa, cosine similarity is computed against all gallery patches: sij = za zb , = . Gallery items are ranked in descending order of similarity, yielding ranked list πi = argsortjG (sij). Relevance definition. Relevance between query and gallery item is encoded by binary indicator yij {0, 1}. In the primary evaluation setting, relevance is defined by spatial correspondence: yij = I(i = j), i.e., the ground-truth match for each query patch is the gallery patch originating from the same spatial location in the same tissue section. 4.4.2 Evaluation metrics Retrieval performance is quantified using top-K metrics derived from the ranked gallery list πi for each query. Top-K accuracy measures whether the correct gallery patch appears within the first retrieved results: Recall@K(i) = (cid:32) t=1 (cid:33) yi,πi(t) > 0 . To assess representation quality under supervised adaptation, we perform patch-level linear probing on frozen embeddings derived from different modalities. To evaluate whether tri-modal alignment enables improved biomarker inference through the integration of metadata-conditioned text and H&E embeddings, we formulate biomarker inference as zero-shot fusion retrieval task. This per-region, per-biomarker PCC captures how well the retrieval-based inference preserves the spatial pattern of biomarker expression across patches within each tissue region. Global and per-biomarker aggregation. Global PCC for each biomarker is computed by averaging PCC (r) across all valid regions: PCCc = 1 Rvalid rRvalid PCC (r) . The aggregate mean PCC across all biomarkers is reported as the primary summary metric. To ensure robustness, only biomarker channels present in at least 80% of evaluation regions are retained, yielding validated set Cvalid of 52 biomarkers.

sections.title: 4.5 Counterfactual retrieval analysis and microenvironment stratification
sections.content: This section defines the downstream analyses used to quantify counterfactual effects induced by metadataonly text-conditioned retrieval. Throughout, we use the notation and embedding definitions from the tri- (with embedding zHE modal retrieval framework. For each fixed H&E query patch xHE ), we construct two metadata-only text descriptions: control description xTXT,(0) and counterfactual description xTXT,(1) , both containing clinical context (e.g., staging, diagnosis, tissue type, survival status) but excluding explicit biomarker-level molecular profiles. The corresponding embeddings zTXT,(0) are obtained by encoding metadata-only descriptions through the pretrained text encoder. For {0, 1}, we form fused query embedding as convex combination, and zTXT,(1) i zfusion,(k) = α zHE + (1 α) zTXT,(k) , We then retrieve the top-K mIF patches from the held-out gallery using cosine similarity: (k) ij = zfusion,(k) zmIF , π (k) = argsortj(s (k) ij ). 32 Linking H&E, mIF, and text in unified framework The retrieved mIF index set under condition is defined as (k) = (cid:110) π (k) (1), . . . , π (k) (K) (cid:111) . All counterfactual analyses below compare the control retrieval set (0) set (1) , while keeping the H&E query patch fixed. against the counterfactual retrieval 4.5.1 Metadata-only text construction for counterfactual analysis To ensure that counterfactual predictions are driven by clinical context rather than explicit biomarker supervision, both control and counterfactual text descriptions are constructed as metadata-only descriptions. Specifically, we apply the same pattern-based truncation procedure described in Methods 4.4.9 to strip all biomarker-specific molecular profile information from the full text descriptions, retaining only tissue-level clinical metadata such as organ type, disease status, diagnosis, staging, grade, survival status, and tissue annotation. For the breast cancer counterfactual experiment, the control description specifies the original staging (T2N0M0, stage IIA, grade 2) while the counterfactual description modifies only the staging fields (T4N2M1, stage IV, grade 3), keeping all other metadata identical. For the lung cancer counterfactual experiment, the control description specifies the original survival status (Deceased, survival: 25 months) while the counterfactual description modifies only the survival fields (Alive, survival: 60 months), keeping all other clinical metadata including staging (T3N1M0, stage IIIA) identical. Both control and counterfactual metadata-only text descriptions are encoded through the pretrained Haiku text encoder and broadcast to all query patches within the selected region, ensuring that all patches share the same text embedding under each condition. For counterfactual retrieval experiments, we use fusion weight of α = 0.6 (H&E) and 1 α = 0.4 (text), which assigns higher weight to the text modality compared with the biomarker inference setting (α = 0.8) to amplify the effect of counterfactual text perturbations on the retrieval outcome. 4.5.2 Patient-level subpopulation composition shift Each retrieved mIF patch is associated with region identifier and corresponding clinical metadata. To quantify whether counterfactual retrieval changes the clinical composition of retrieved patches, we analyze TNM-derived categories associated with the retrieved region identifiers. Let ℓ(j) denote the TNM string associated with retrieved patch (when available). We parse ℓ(j) into its component categories and perform analyses independently for and stages. For category label set (e.g., = {N0, N1, N2, . . .}), we compute, for each query i, condition k, and category Y, the within-query proportion pi,k(c) = 1 (k) jC(k) I(ℓ(j) = c) . For each category c, we compare the paired distributions {pi,0(c)}i and {pi,1(c)}i using paired two-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum (MannWhitney U) test across the matched queries. Multiple testing across categories is controlled using the BenjaminiHochberg false discovery rate (FDR) procedure. 4.5.3 H&E embedding-based microenvironment clustering To stratify query patches by morphological context, we perform unsupervised clustering on Haiku H&E embeddings. For selected tissue region, we collect the normalized H&E embeddings {zHE i=1 and apply K-means clustering with fixed number of clusters Kclust = 4, yielding cluster assignments ci }n Linking H&E, mIF, and text in unified framework {0, 1, . . . , Kclust 1} and cluster centroids µk. Each resulting cluster is then assigned descriptive morphological compartment label (e.g., fibroblast-rich stroma, epithelial-dominant tumor core) by manual inspection of representative prototype patches (Methods 4.5.4). 4.5.4 Prototype patch selection for cluster interpretation To facilitate qualitative interpretation of each H&E-derived cluster, we select representative prototype patches. For each cluster k, we compute Euclidean distances between cluster members and the cluster centroid in embedding space and select the closest patches, with = 3 when available: Pk = arg min i: ci=k Pk=m (cid:13) (cid:13)zHE (cid:13) µk (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13)2 . The corresponding H&E image crops are saved and used as visual prototypes for manual cluster annotation, in which each H&E-derived cluster is assigned descriptive morphological compartment name (e.g., fibroblast-rich stroma, inflamed tumor zone, epithelial-dominant tumor core) by inspection of these prototype crops. 4.5.5 Cluster-stratified biomarker differential testing under counterfactual retrieval To quantify counterfactual biomarker shifts under fixed morphological context, we perform clusterstratified analysis conditioned on H&E-derived clusters. Specifically, for given H&E cluster g, we restrict attention to query patches whose embeddings are assigned to cluster and compare biomarker abundance patterns between the original and counterfactual retrieval results. Let Ig = {i ci = g} denote the set of query patches assigned to H&E cluster g. For each query patch Ig, metadata-only fusion-based retrieval yields two mIF patch sets: (0) (original condition), (1) (counterfactual condition). Weighted mean biomarker abundance summarization. For each retrieved mIF patch and biomarker channel Cvalid, we obtain the mean biomarker abundance µj,c from the associated spatial biomarker quantification. For each query patch i, condition {0, 1}, and biomarker c, we summarize biomarker abundance over the top-K retrieved mIF patches using similarity-score-weighted averaging: µ (k) i,c = jC(k) (k) ij µj,c jC(k) (k) ij , (k) ij denotes the fusion retrieval similarity score for patch under condition k. This score-weighted where formulation assigns higher influence to more confidently retrieved patches. Counterfactual abundance shift. For each query patch Ig and biomarker c, we define the counterfactual abundance shift as di,c = µ (1) i,c µ (0) i,c . Positive values of di,c indicate increased biomarker abundance under the counterfactual condition, while negative values indicate decreased abundance. 34 Linking H&E, mIF, and text in unified framework Statistical testing within clusters. For each biomarker Cvalid, we test whether the distribution {di,c}iIg differs significantly from zero using two-sided Wilcoxon signed-rank test, which accounts for paired comparisons at the query level. To control for multiple testing across biomarkers, p-values are adjusted using the BenjaminiHochberg false discovery rate (FDR) procedure. This cluster-stratified, abundance-based testing framework isolates molecular shifts attributable to counterfactual metadata-only semantic intervention under fixed H&E morphology, while the score-weighted averaging ensures that retrieval confidence is appropriately accounted for in the biomarker summarization. 4.5.6 PCA of per-patch counterfactual biomarker shift profiles To quantify heterogeneity of counterfactual biomarker abundance changes within fixed microenvironment cluster, we assemble the per-patch shift vectors into shift matrix bi = (di,1, di,2, . . . , di,C) RC, Ig, = [bi]iIg RIgC. Biomarker channels with missing values for all patches are removed. To enable PCA without imputation, patches with incomplete shift vectors after channel filtering are excluded. We then perform PCA on and obtain 2D coordinates for each patch: ui = PCA(bi) R2, ui = (ui,1, ui,2). The resulting PCA projection summarizes dominant modes of variation in counterfactual biomarker shifts across patches. 4.5.7 Association between baseline biomarker state and counterfactual shift trajectories Finally, we test whether baseline biomarker state is associated with the dominant axes of counterfactual change. For each query patch Ig, we compute the baseline biomarker vector from the original matched mIF patch xmIF orig,i (i.e., the mIF patch aligned to the fixed H&E query prior to retrieval). Baseline biomarker means are computed as morig i,c = 1 P2 h=1 w=1 xmIF orig,i(h, w, c), = 1, . . . , C. We then compute Pearson correlation coefficients between {morig and {ui,2}iIg : i,c }iIg and the PCA coordinates {ui,1}iIg (cid:16) ρc,1 = corr {morig i,c }iIg , {ui,1}iIg (cid:17) , (cid:16) ρc,2 = corr {morig i,c }iIg , {ui,2}iIg (cid:17) . These correlations are summarized as biomarker-by-PC correlation matrix.
